summary: Stancil argues that elite institutions and Silicon Valley share a performative culture that rewards confident, untested ideas rather than rigorous scrutiny, producing a class of leaders who are affirmed rather than challenged. He sees the VC pitch dynamic and Twitter’s Musk-era chaos as parallel examples of the “very smart boy” myth and its consequences. Twitter becomes the live-fire test of whether tech’s celebrated first‑principles bravado is wisdom or just noise.
money_quotes: They were never told that they were wrong, only that they were interesting.
Most of these suggestions are naked copies of industry fads or the VC's latest successes.

But if it tears itself down—if Twitter ferments into another toxic backwater, overrun with misinformation, frothing violence, and scams and scammy ads, as so many are warning it will—Silicon Valley’s reputation should go with it.
The ugly experiment at Twitter is becoming the ultimate test of tech industry dogma, and of entrepreneurs’ supposed golden touch.
Our emperors have no shortage of confidence; now we’ll find out if they have any clothes.
key_insight: The tech elite’s confidence and folklore about visionary genius are being stress‑tested in public, and Twitter may expose how shallow those claims really are.
